jokes.text: Homer gets stuck under a toy that keeps repeating 'I like to play with you' while his back is injured
jokes.setup: Homer injures his back stepping on Bart's toys
jokes.punchline: Gets trapped under a repetitive talking toy, helplessly repeating the phrase
jokes.explanation: Classic physical comedy with escalating repetition. The contrast between Homer's pain and the cheerful toy creates sustained laughs.

jokes.text: Clumsy Homer.
jokes.setup: Homer is injured and blaming Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart dismissively calls his father clumsy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart - deflecting responsibility with casual insult, maximum impact in two words

jokes.text: Everything's always my fault. If he'd just watch out where he was going.
jokes.setup: Bart caused the accident by leaving toys around
jokes.punchline: Blames Homer for not watching where he's going
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - deflecting blame with twisted logic. Well-integrated character comedy.

jokes.text: Mother, please don't call me Spanky on school grounds.
jokes.punchline: Reveals his embarrassing childhood nickname in front of students
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character development - humanizes the authoritarian Skinner with a mortifying detail. Sets up running gag perfectly.

jokes.text: Bye, Spanky.
jokes.setup: Skinner asked her not to use the nickname
jokes.punchline: She immediately does it again as she leaves
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback timing and economy. Two words maximum impact, builds the running gag.

jokes.text: So long, sucker.
jokes.setup: Bart pretends to go to the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Actually setting up his prank, addressing the toilet
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart moment - the misdirection works and the line delivery is perfect for his character.

jokes.text: Now hold it right there, you little-- Mother?
jokes.setup: Skinner chasing Bart after the explosion
jokes.punchline: Discovers his mother was affected by the prank
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of consequences. The abrupt shift from authority to concern creates great comedy timing.

jokes.text: Make sure it's squished flat and crunchy on the outside... And maybe some of those little wieners that come in a can?
jokes.setup: Homer asking for a grilled cheese sandwich
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific and unappetizing food preferences
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Homer's working-class tastes and neediness. Specific details make it funnier.

jokes.text: Hello, Principal Skinner. I'd get up, but the boy crippled me.
jokes.setup: Homer injured his back on Bart's toys
jokes.punchline: Dramatically exaggerates while blaming Bart
jokes.explanation: Great callback to opening sequence. Homer's melodramatic victimhood while throwing Bart under the bus is perfect characterization.

jokes.text: I think it behooves us all to consider... deportation.
jokes.punchline: Jumps to international deportation for a school prank
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from normal school discipline. The formal language makes the absurdity funnier.

jokes.text: Deportation? You mean kick Bart out of the county?
jokes.punchline: Marge misunderstands as 'county' not 'country'
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that shows Marge's limited worldview. Works as character moment more than pure joke.

jokes.text: Hear him out, Marge.
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately supports getting rid of his son
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - immediately jumping on the chance to be rid of Bart. Economy and character integration are flawless.

jokes.text: Sounds great. Although, a kid can't learn much in just three months.
jokes.setup: Discussing educational benefits of exchange program
jokes.punchline: Homer focuses on wrong aspect entirely
jokes.explanation: Homer completely missing the point about removing Bart. Good character comedy showing his priorities.

jokes.text: Yeah, but what about Bart?
jokes.setup: Skinner says average child becomes fluent quickly
jokes.punchline: Homer questions if this applies to his below-average son
jokes.explanation: Great callback to Bart's poor academic performance. Homer's parental honesty is brutally funny.

jokes.text: You mean all white with pink eyes?
jokes.setup: Told they'd get an Albanian student
jokes.punchline: Homer confuses Albanian with albino
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer ignorance moment. The specific detail about pink eyes makes it funnier and more believable.

jokes.text: The life of a frog. That's the life for me.
jokes.setup: Bart playing with his frog at episode opening
jokes.punchline: Ironically foreshadows his upcoming French misery
jokes.explanation: Clever ironic foreshadowing since he'll be miserable in France. Works better on rewatch when you know his fate.

jokes.text: He makes me crazy 12 months a year. At least you get the summer off.
jokes.setup: Talking to Skinner about dealing with Bart
jokes.punchline: Envies teachers who get breaks from kids
jokes.explanation: Relatable parental frustration delivered with Homer's characteristic selfishness. Good observational humor.

jokes.text: Yes, baby! Way to go!
jokes.setup: Bart agrees to go to France
jokes.punchline: Homer celebrates getting rid of his son
jokes.explanation: Homer's enthusiastic reaction to Bart leaving is perfectly in character and darkly funny.

jokes.text: Don't mess up France the way you messed up your room.
jokes.punchline: Compares an entire country to Bart's messy room
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback to the opening chaos. Homer's scale confusion is perfect - treating a country like a bedroom.

jokes.text: Eww. What a dump.
jokes.punchline: Arrives at rundown, depressing location
jokes.explanation: Standard expectation vs reality gag. Works but not particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: You gotta be kiddin'. The lek.
jokes.punchline: Homer's incredulous reaction to foreign money name
jokes.explanation: Homer's American-centric worldview on display. His delivery sells the incredulity well.

jokes.text: Political what?
jokes.setup: Adil mentions 'furious political thought' as export
jokes.punchline: Homer can't process the concept
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's anti-intellectual nature and political ignorance.

jokes.text: Escape is impossible.
jokes.punchline: Immediately establishes it's more like a prison
jokes.explanation: Perfectly ominous welcome that immediately subverts expectations. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Affectionate little Albanian, isn't he?
jokes.punchline: Homer misreads spy behavior as genuine affection
jokes.explanation: Works better on rewatch when you know Adil is a spy. Homer's obliviousness to manipulation is in character.

jokes.text: In this way, and only in this way, can we hope to better understand our backward neighbours throughout the world.
jokes.punchline: Immediately calls other cultures 'backward'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of American cultural condescension. Skinner's pompous language makes the hypocrisy funnier.

jokes.text: Although I have only been in your county a few days, I have already found Americans to be most... trusting.
jokes.punchline: Subtly reveals he's taking advantage of their naivety
jokes.explanation: Excellent double meaning that works on multiple levels. The pause before 'trusting' is perfectly timed menace.

jokes.text: Although, officially, I am required to hate you, I want you to know I do not feel it in my heart.
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions official hatred policy
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny revelation of Cold War tensions delivered as casual conversation. The matter-of-fact tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: How can you defend a country where 5% of the people control 95% of the wealth?
jokes.punchline: Child casually citing communist talking points
jokes.explanation: Good political satire showing indoctrination. The casualness of revolutionary rhetoric from a child is effectively absurd.

jokes.text: Maybe Adil has a point about the machinery of capitalism being oiled with the blood of the workers.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to mediate the argument
jokes.punchline: Accidentally endorses communist rhetoric
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment - trying to be diplomatic but accidentally spouting Marxist theory. Perfect character obliviousness.

jokes.text: No, no, Mrs. Simpson. You have been oppressed enough for today.
jokes.punchline: Adil frames housework as oppression
jokes.explanation: Smart political humor - communist rhetoric applied to domestic tasks. Shows Adil's ideological conditioning.

jokes.text: We've always blamed ourselves, but I guess it's pretty clear which cylinder wasn't firing.
jokes.setup: Homer comparing Adil favorably to Bart
jokes.punchline: Uses car metaphor to call Bart defective
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer metaphor - mechanical thinking applied to family. The casual cruelty toward Bart is darkly funny.

jokes.text: Your paper-thin commitment to your children sends shivers down my spine.
jokes.punchline: Lisa calls out Homer's terrible parenting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa moment - articulate criticism of family dysfunction. Her mature language makes it funnier.

jokes.text: And if she doesn't, we can always exchange her.
jokes.punchline: Homer suggests trading Lisa away too
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny extension of the exchange program concept. Homer's willingness to trade all his kids is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Can I have something to go with my turnip?
jokes.punchline: Single turnip as entire meal
jokes.explanation: Standard deprivation gag. The single turnip is visually funny but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Quiet! When you work like a man, we will feed you like one.
jokes.punchline: Child labor exploitation rationalized
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about child exploitation. Works as villainous characterization but not especially clever.

jokes.text: Aw! You called me Dad.
jokes.punchline: Homer is more touched by this than his real son ever made him
jokes.explanation: Homer's emotional neediness satisfied by foreign spy child

jokes.text: None of my biological kids ever wanted to see me at work.
jokes.setup: Adil wants to visit the power plant
jokes.punchline: Homer casually mentions his kids' disinterest
jokes.explanation: Sad-funny moment showing Homer's obliviousness to why his kids avoid him. The term 'biological kids' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: See these? American doughnuts. Glazed, powdered and raspberry-filled. Now how's that for freedom of choice?
jokes.setup: Homer showing off America to Adil
jokes.punchline: Equates freedom with donut varieties
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of American consumer culture. Homer's proud delivery of shallow materialism is perfect.

jokes.text: Hey, Lenny. Does this place have one of those plutonium isolation deals?
jokes.punchline: Homer casually asks coworker about nuclear materials
jokes.explanation: Homer's casual attitude toward nuclear security

jokes.text: Yeah, over in sector 12... Third floor, by the candy machines.
jokes.punchline: Dangerous nuclear material stored casually near snacks
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist humor about nuclear safety. The casual treatment of dangerous materials is perfectly Springfield.

jokes.text: Ungrateful swine! We give you food, we give you shelter, and this is how you repay us! You little shutterbug.
jokes.setup: Cesar angry at Bart for taking photos
jokes.punchline: Calls him 'shutterbug' as an insult
jokes.explanation: The shift from angry ranting to 'shutterbug' as an insult is mildly amusing but not particularly strong.

jokes.text: Bunch of creeps! I hate France!
jokes.punchline: Condemns entire country based on two criminals
jokes.explanation: Typical Bart overgeneralization from limited bad experience

jokes.text: Do not worry. This is France. It is customary for children to take a little wine now and then.
jokes.setup: Forcing Bart to drink suspicious liquid
jokes.punchline: Uses French culture to justify giving alcohol to children
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about child endangerment disguised as cultural education. The casual justification is effectively creepy.

jokes.text: But you gotta help me. These two guys I'm staying with, they work me day and night. They don't feed me.
jokes.setup: Bart desperately trying to get help from French policeman
jokes.punchline: Officer doesn't understand English, can't help
jokes.explanation: Standard language barrier comedy. Creates sympathy for Bart but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Here I've listened to nothing but French for the past... Sacré bleu! Where is me mama?
jokes.setup: Bart frustrated about not learning French
jokes.punchline: Suddenly speaks French without realizing it
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to earlier setup about language immersion. Bart's unconscious French acquisition is a great character moment.

jokes.text: I bet he could build a nuclear power plant if he wanted to.
jokes.punchline: Unknowingly references exactly what Adil is doing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant irony - Homer's innocent praise perfectly describes Adil's espionage goals. Great dramatic irony.

jokes.text: All right. Well, the name of his county starts with the letter 'A.'
jokes.punchline: Immediately spills classified information
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke about government competence. The quick reversal from secrecy to blabbing is funny.

jokes.text: Adil? The Sparrow!
jokes.punchline: Homer realizes it's his exchange student
jokes.explanation: Perfect revelation moment with great economy. Homer's realization and the dramatic spy name reveal work perfectly together.

jokes.text: The Sparrow!
jokes.punchline: Agent realizes Adil is the spy they're hunting
jokes.explanation: Major plot revelation moment with dramatic impact

jokes.text: Au revoir, suckers.
jokes.punchline: Uses Bart's catchphrase but in French
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to Bart's line but with spy twist. The French version adds linguistic sophistication to the insult.

jokes.text: Sometimes I think I am getting too old for this game.
jokes.setup: Adil being exchanged for American spy
jokes.punchline: Child spy using tired action movie cliché
jokes.explanation: Good parody of spy movie tropes. The absurdity of a child using the clichéd line works well.

jokes.text: Good-bye, Adil. I'll send you those civil defense plans you wanted.
jokes.setup: Homer saying goodbye to the revealed spy
jokes.punchline: Still doesn't understand and offers more classified information
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer obliviousness capper. Even after learning Adil is a spy, he still offers to help him. Brilliant character consistency.

jokes.text: So, basically, I met one nice French person.
jokes.punchline: Understates his traumatic experience with dry observation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart deadpan response to his ordeal. The massive understatement after all his suffering is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Sorry, Marge. Some wise guy stuck a cork in the bottle.
jokes.setup: Marge wants to try Bart's French wine
jokes.punchline: Homer doesn't understand how wine bottles work
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer ignorance moment. Treating a cork as sabotage rather than standard wine packaging is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You hear that, Marge? My boy speaks French.
jokes.punchline: Homer acts like Bart is fluent
jokes.explanation: Good callback to language learning theme. Homer's parental pride over minimal achievement is nicely in character.
